elements.sent-2411-001: Our method produces visually compelling reconstructions of constituent face images compared to previous approaches in Scenario 1.
elements.sent-2411-002: The generated outputs are i) distinct from each other and ii) noticeably different from the original morph.. \end{center ].}
elements.sent-2411-003: A facial morph is an image created by combining two face images pertaining to two distinct identities.
elements.sent-2411-004: Face demorphing inverts the process and tries to recover the original images constituting a facial morph.
elements.sent-2411-005: While morph attack detection (MAD) techniques can be used to flag morph images, they do not divulge any visual information about the faces used to create them.
elements.sent-2411-007: Existing demorphing techniques are either very restrictive (assume identities during testing) or produce feeble outputs (both outputs look very similar).
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper, we overcome these issues by proposing dc-GAN, a novel GAN-based demorphing method conditioned on the morph images.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our method overcomes morph-replication and produces high quality reconstructions of the bonafide images used to create the morphs.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, our method is highly generalizable across demorphing paradigms (differential/reference-free).
elements.sent-2411-011: We conduct experiments on AMSL, FRLL-Morphs and MorDiff datasets to showcase the efficacy of our method.
elements.sent-2411-012: Code and train-test split is available at hidden for blind review.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.7\columnwidth]{images/WACV-arch-3.png} \caption{Dual-Conditioned GAN for Reference-Free Demorphing: An image encoder $\mathcal{E}$ encodes the morph image, which is then used as a condition in the generator. The generator, based on a UNet architecture $\mathcal{G}$, takes in the MORPH image and the encoded representation $\mathcal{E}(MORPH)$, producing two outputs, OUT1 and OUT2. The discriminator is trained to distinguish between the real set (MORPH, BF1, BF2) and the synthetic set (MORPH, OUT1, OUT2), differentiating real from synthetic pairs.} \label{fig:arch}
elements.sent-2411-013: Morphing, in the biometric literature, refers to the blending of two or more face images to generate a composite image that matches the identities if the images used to create it.
elements.sent-2411-014: This matching is with respect to an automated Face Recognition System (FRS).
elements.sent-2411-015: Such a composite image, known as a facial morph, can be incorporated into a biometric-based identity document thereby allowing multiple individuals to use the same document.
elements.sent-2411-017: In October 2018, German activists obtained a legitimate German passport by using a morphed picture of a European Union official and a member of their group\cite{ref12 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Historically, morphs were created by extracting facial landmarks from face images and then combining the two face images \cite{ref23,ref21,ref50 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, more recently, deep learning models have been remarkably successful in generating high-quality morphs, especially, those based on generative paradigms like diffusion models \cite{ref9 and generative adversarial attacks \cite{ref13,ref14,ref20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Morph Attack Detection (MAD) is essential for retaining the integrity, security and reliability of FRS.
elements.sent-2411-021: Current MAD techniques can be broadly divided into two categories: i) reference-based differential-image technique \cite{ref16,ref53 , and ii) reference-free single-image techniques \cite{ref18,ref51,ref41 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: While reference-based techniques compare the input image to a reference image, which is usually a trusted live image of the person taken under a trusted acquisition scenario, reference-free methods use facial features extracted from the input to determine whether or not the input is morphed.
elements.sent-2411-023: While MAD techniques can reliably flag facial morphs, they do not reveal the images involved in the creation of the morph.
elements.sent-2411-024: From a forensic standpoint, it is valuable to recover images of the constituent identities\footnote{The constituent images are referred to as ``bonafides" since in some literature, they are real images of real people.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This process of extracting constituent face images from a morph image is called face demorphing.
elements.sent-2411-026: Limited work exists in this field, especially in reference-free face demorphing.
elements.sent-2411-027: In this paper, we focus on both differential as well as reference-free face demorphing.
elements.sent-2411-028: To set up the problem, we first identify multiple scenarios that are plausible in the real world.
elements.sent-2411-029: In the context of this work, the choice of train and test data will define these scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-030: To formally define the various scenarios arising in real life, let $\mathcal{X _{train}$, $\mathcal{Y _{train}$ denote the training data corresponding to the morphs and bonafides, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Assume that all morph images in $\mathcal{X _{train}$ images are necessarily generated only using the bonafide identities in $\mathcal{Y _{train}$. \begin{equation}\forall x\in \mathcal{X}_{train}, \mathcal{M}(y_1,y_2)=x \Rightarrow \{y_1,y_2\}\in \mathcal{Y}_{train}\end{equation} where, $\mathcal{M}$ is the morphing operator.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: We now define three scenarios: i) Train and test morphs are obtained from the same pool of bonafide identities.
elements.sent-2411-034: These conditions can be formally defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{Scenario 1)} \ \ \ \ \ \ \mathcal{Y}_{test}\subseteq\mathcal{Y}_{train} , \ \mathcal{X}_{train} \cap \mathcal{X}_{test}=\phi \text{Scenario 2)}\ \ \ \ \ \ \mathcal{Y}_{test}\subset\mathcal{Y}_{train} , \ \mathcal{X}_{train} \cap \mathcal{X}_{test}=\phi \text{Scenario 3})\ \mathcal{Y}_{test}\cap\mathcal{Y}_{train}\ =\ \ \mathcal{X}_{train} \cap \mathcal{X}_{test}=\phi \end{aligned} \label{eq:scenario}\end{equation} Most of the previous work assumed scenario 1 implicitly \cite{ref18,ref66 , but this is a strong assumption hampering the generalisability of the demorphing technique.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} Scenario 1) \ \ \ \ \ \ Y_{test}\subseteqY_{train} , \ X_{train} \cap X_{test}=\phi Scenario 2)\ \ \ \ \ \ Y_{test}\subsetY_{train} , \ X_{train} \cap X_{test}=\phi Scenario 3)\ Y_{test}\capY_{train}\ =\ \ X_{train} \cap X_{test}=\phi  \label{eq:scenario}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-035: Limited research has been conducted on demorphing in Scenario 3 \cite{ref51 , and the results often replicate the morph in both the outputs (see Figure \ref{fig:vis_comparison ).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: In this work, we propose a demorphing technique free of any assumption regarding the identities of images (scenario 3) as well as the availability of a reference image (differential/reference-free).
elements.sent-2411-037: In summary, our contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-038: We propose dc-GAN, a novel GAN-based technique designed to extract identities from a single morph.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our approach is well-suited for both reference-free and differential demorphing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-040: We conduct a thorough analysis of the implicit bias present in morphs and extensively benchmark publicly available face recognition systems (FRS) on demorphing tasks in the presence of visible artefacts.
elements.sent-2411-041: To the best of our knowledge, our method is the first to overcome morph-replication on unseen identities (scenario 3).
elements.sent-2411-042: Face Morphing refers to the process of combining two or more face images corresponding to two distinct identities to produce another face image (called a morph) which has a high biometric similarity (or less biometric distance), as assessed by an automated face matcher, with both identities.
elements.sent-2411-043: Typically, a complex morphing operator, $\mathcal{M}$, acts upon the input bonafides $\mathcal{I _1$ and $\mathcal{I _2$ to produce the morph $\mathcal{X}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{X}=\mathcal{M}(\mathcal{I}_1,\mathcal{I}_2)\end{equation} The goal of $\mathcal{M}$ is to ensure that $\mathcal{B (\mathcal{I _1,\mathcal{X )>\tau$ and $\mathcal{B (\mathcal{I _2,\mathcal{X )>\tau$ where $\mathcal{B}$ is a biometric face matcher that produces a similarity score and $\tau$ is the similarity threshold.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Face demorphing is the inverse of the morphing process.
elements.sent-2411-045: Given a morph $\mathcal{X}$, the goal is to recover the face images used to produce $\mathcal{X}$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Initial work on demorphing were primarily reference-based, i.e., they required an image of one of the identities to recover the other \cite{ref16,9484355 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The authors also assumed prior knowledge about the morphing factor used during the morphing process.
elements.sent-2411-048: FD-GAN \cite{ref17 is a reference-based method which uses a dual architecture and attempts to recover an image if the first identity from the morphed input using an image of the second identity.}
elements.sent-2411-049: To validate the effectiveness of its generative model, it then tries to recover the second identity using the output of the first identity.
elements.sent-2411-050: More recently, reference-free demporphing techniques have also been proposed with some degree of success.
elements.sent-2411-051: In \cite{ref51 , the authors decompose the morph image into two output images using a GAN that is composed of a generator, a decomposition critic, and two Markovian discriminators.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Shukla \cite{ref18 proposed a diffusion-based method for scenario 1 that iteratively adds noise to the morph image and recovers the constituent face images during the backward process.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In \cite{ref66 , the authors decompose the morph into multiple privacy-preserving components using an encoder, and then recover the constituent face images by weighting and combining these components with a decoder.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In \cite{ref51 , the authors attempt to recover identities from a single image using a GAN \cite{ref32 that includes a generator for demorphing and three Markovian discriminators.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: However, the method has a problem, where the outputs not only closely resemble the morph image but also appear similar to each other (See Figure \ref{fig:vis_comparison ), we term this phenomenon as morph-replication.}
elements.sent-2411-056: This issue arises because the morphing operator $\mathcal{M}$ is a highly nonlinear and complex function, making demorphing an ill-posed problem.
elements.sent-2411-057: Although their method employs three discriminators to optimize output similarity and visual realism, the generator only receives the morph image, which provides insufficient guidance for the demorphing process.
elements.sent-2411-058: The morph image alone may not be adequate to recover both ground-truth face images due to the biases introduced during the morphing process.
elements.sent-2411-059: Inspired by their work, our method also utilizes GAN, but with a key difference: we introduce conditioning on both the generator and discriminator.
elements.sent-2411-060: This conditioning provides more robust guidance for the generation of constituent images.
elements.sent-2411-061: We illustrate the architecture of our method in Figure \ref{fig:arch .}
elements.sent-2411-062: A UNet \cite{ref19 generator $\mathcal{G}$, based on an encoder-decoder architecture receives the morph input and generates two outputs.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Simultaneously, an image encoder $\mathcal{E}$ encodes the morph image and feeds the resulting embedding into the generator.
elements.sent-2411-064: This morph embedding is injected into the intermediate latent layers of the generator.
elements.sent-2411-065: We refer to our method as a dual-conditioned GAN (dc-GAN), conditioned on (i) the morph image in the image domain and (ii) morph image embeddings in the latent domain.
elements.sent-2411-066: The discriminator receives a ``real'' triplet, consisting of the morph image along with the ground truth images, and a ``fake'' triplet, composed of the morph image and the two generated outputs.
elements.sent-2411-067: The discriminator is then trained in an adversarial fashion alongside the generator to distinguish the real triplet from the synthetically generated one.
elements.sent-2411-068: Note that we also condition the discriminator with the morph image, providing it with the context needed to distinguish the morph from the constituent ground-truth images.
elements.sent-2411-069: The conditions work in tandem to ensure that morph-replication does not occur.
elements.sent-2411-070: For differential demorphing, we modify our pipeline slightly.
elements.sent-2411-071: The image encoder now encodes both the morph image and the reference image, concatenating them to provide guidance for the generator.
elements.sent-2411-072: The generator receives both the morph and reference images as input (with 6 channels) and outputs the reconstructed image (with 3 channels corresponding to RGB).
elements.sent-2411-073: The discriminator architecture and function remain unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-074: The loss function for dc-GAN can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\min_{G}\max_{D} L_{dcGAN}(D,G) = E_{(x,y)\sim p_{data}(\cdot)}[\log D(y|x)] + E_{z\sim p_z(z), (x,y)\sim p_{data}(\cdot) }[\log(1-D(G(z|x,E(x))|x)]\end{equation} The generator is conditioned on the morph, $x$, and its representation, $\mathcal{E (x)$.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\min_{G}\max_{D} L_{dcGAN}(D,G) = E_{(x,y)\sim p_{data}(\cdot)}[\log D(y|x)] + E_{z\sim p_z(z), (x,y)\sim p_{data}(\cdot) }[\log(1-D(G(z|x,E(x))|x)]\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: We denote the bonafide image-pairs as y.
elements.sent-2411-076: Similarly, the discriminator is conditioned on the morph as well.
elements.sent-2411-077: We introduce noise exclusively through dropout, which is applied to several layers of our generator during both training and test phases, following the approach suggested in \cite{ref72 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: However, previous approaches observed that generating face images without explicit guidance results in blurry and less discriminatory features \cite{ref73 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: On this account, we add another component to the loss function, along with the standard dc-GAN loss, which guides the model to generate feature-rich faces.
elements.sent-2411-080: Another issue is the lack of order in the generator's outputs (outputs are generated in no specific order).
elements.sent-2411-081: To address this, we align the generator’s outputs with the ground-truth face images using the widely-employed cross-road loss \cite{ref51 . \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{cr}= \begin{cases} \begin{aligned} \min [\mathcal{L}_1({\mathcal{I}}_1,\mathcal{O}_1)+ \mathcal{L}_1({\mathcal{I}_2},\mathcal{O}_2), \mathcal{L}_1({\mathcal{I}_1},\mathcal{O}_2)+ \mathcal{L}_1({\mathcal{I}_2},\mathcal{O}_1) ] \end{aligned} if \ \text{reference free} \mathcal{L}_1(\mathcal{I},\mathcal{O}) if \ \text{differential} \end{cases} \label{cr_loss}\end{equation} where, $\mathcal{L _1$ is the standard per-pixel loss, $\{\mathcal{I _{1 ,\mathcal{I _2\}$ are the ground-truth face images and $\{\mathcal{O _{1 ,\mathcal{O _2\}$ are the generated outputs from our method.}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}L_{cr}=   \min [L_1({I}_1,O_1)+ L_1({I_2},O_2), L_1({I_1},O_2)+ L_1({I_2},O_1) ]  if \ reference free L_1(I,O) if \ differential  \label{cr_loss}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-082: In the case of reference-free demorphing from a single morph, the model generates two images, which are compared against the two ground-truth face images.
elements.sent-2411-083: The cross-road loss matches the correct output to the ground truth by computing the $\mathcal{L _1$ loss between all possible pairs (only two).}
elements.sent-2411-084: Taking the minimum ensures that the correct match is selected.
elements.sent-2411-085: For differential demorphing, the cross-road loss simplifies to the $\mathcal{L _1$ loss between the single output $\mathcal{O}$, and the single ground truth, $\mathcal{I}$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The final loss is then given by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}= \mathcal{L}_{dcGAN}(\mathcal{G},\mathcal{D})+\alpha \mathbb{E}_{x\sim p(\mathcal{X})}[\mathcal{L}_{cr}(\mathcal{O},\mathcal{G}(x,\mathcal{E}(x)))] \label{loss}\end{equation} where, $\mathcal{L _1$ is the standard per-pixel loss, $x\sim p(\mathcal{X )$ is the input morph and $\mathcal{E}$ is the image encoder.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Demorphing: Our pipeline consists of three components: i) an image encoder $\mathcal{E}$, ii) a generator $\mathcal{G}$, and iii) a discriminator $\mathcal{D}$.
elements.sent-2411-089: For $\mathcal{E}$, we use a CLIP-based \cite{ref74 image encoder to effectively process and represent facial information.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The choice of CLIP over face-specific models is due to its general-purpose utility.
elements.sent-2411-091: Since morphs often contain high-frequency artefacts and overlapping features, a general-purpose encoder like CLIP is more robust and offers better representation than models trained solely on real faces.
elements.sent-2411-092: We use CLIP-ViT-B/32 model as a morph encoder which is based on the transformer architecture.
elements.sent-2411-093: The 32 in ViT-B/32 refers to the size of the image patches.
elements.sent-2411-094: In this model, each image is divided into 32$\times$32 pixel patches.
elements.sent-2411-095: These patches are then linearly embedded into a sequence of tokens that are processed by the transformer layers.
elements.sent-2411-096: We use a pretrained CLIP model from the HuggingFace library, which produces embeddings of size 1$\times$512.
elements.sent-2411-097: We repeat the embedding 77 times, mimicking the practice used in Stable Diffusion \cite{ref75 .}
elements.sent-2411-098: For the generator, we use HuggingFace implementation of conditional UNet, namely, $UNet2DConditionModel$ featuring 6 ResNet downsampling blocks and an equal number of ResNet upsampling blocks.
elements.sent-2411-099: Self-attention is implemented in the fifth downsampling block and the second upsampling block, allowing the model to focus on important spatial features.
elements.sent-2411-100: Along with the image, the UNet also receives morph embeddings, which are injected into intermediate layers of both the downsampling and upsampling blocks.
elements.sent-2411-101: This integration allows the outputs to retain context about the original morph image throughout the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-102: This UNet implementation also requires a timestep, which we have consistently set to zero in all our experiments, effectively eliminating its impact.
elements.sent-2411-103: Finally, our Discriminator is based on a CNN architecture consisting of four blocks, each containing a convolutional layer, InstanceNorm \cite{ref76 , and LeakyReLU.}
elements.sent-2411-104: The discriminator concatenates the real and synthetic triplets along the channel axis and outputs a real/synthetic score.
elements.sent-2411-105: Face Recognition Models and Parameters: We thoroughly evaluate our demorphing method using five publicly available Face Recognition Models: AdaFace \cite{ref22 , ArcFace \cite{ref77 , VGG-Face \cite{ref78 , FaceNet \cite{ref79 , and OpenFace \cite{ref80 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: We evaluate the performance of each FRS in the presence of high-frequency artefacts introduced during the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-107: While we benchmark five different FRSs, we use ArcFace as the primary FRS, consistent with its use in \cite{ref51 .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Each FRS is employed with its default settings as provided by the authors.
elements.sent-2411-109: We use cosine similarity to compute the biometric match score.
elements.sent-2411-110: Training is conducted using Adam optimization \cite{ref60 with multi-GPU support via accelerate \cite{ref81 .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: The training parameters are as follows: number of epochs: 300, learning rate: $10^{-4}$, dropout rate=0.1, $\beta_1$=0.5 and $\beta_2$=0.999.

elements.sent-2411-112: We conduct our experiments on three well-known morph datasets: AMSL \cite{ref64 , FRLL-Morphs \cite{ref65 , and MorDiff \cite{ref9 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: The FRLL-Morphs dataset includes morphs generated using four different techniques: OpenCV \cite{ref67 , StyleGAN \cite{ref69 , WebMorph \cite{ref70 , and FaceMorph \cite{ref68 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-114: In all three datasets, the bonafides are derived from the FRLL dataset, which comprises 102 identities, each with two frontal images (one smiling and one neutral).
elements.sent-2411-115: The morph counts in each of the dataset are as follows: AMSL: 2,175 morphs; FaceMorpher: 1,222 morphs; StyleGAN: 1,222 morphs; OpenCV: 1,221 morphs; WebMorph: 1,221 morphs; MorDiff: 1,000 morphs.
elements.sent-2411-116: To split the dataset into training and testing sets, we follow an identity-disjoint protocol.
elements.sent-2411-117: We divide the 102 identities into training and testing sets using a 60-40 split.
elements.sent-2411-118: A morph image is included in the training (or test) set only if both bonafide identities used to create it belong to the training (or test) set.
elements.sent-2411-119: Morphs where one identity belongs to the training set and the other to the test set (or vice versa) are excluded.
elements.sent-2411-120: Additionally, any identities that do not participate in morph generation are also discarded.
elements.sent-2411-121: To maintain consistency across datasets, we only consider neutral faces (AMSL morphs are exclusively generated from neutral facial expressions bonafides).
elements.sent-2411-122: Intrinsic Bias in Morphs: Figure \ref{fig:hard-samples shows randomly selected examples from the AMSL dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-123: Typically, a morph is created by superimposing a face image onto a base image.
elements.sent-2411-124: This process inherently introduces a bias toward the base image, making it extremely difficult to recover the other identity from the morph.
elements.sent-2411-125: To quantify this bias, we compute the similarity distributions between the morph and the two ground-truth images (MORPH-BF1 and MORPH-BF2) and calculate the $d'$ values for each in Table \ref{tab:dprime .}
elements.sent-2411-126: A higher $d'$ value indicates that the distributions are well separated.
elements.sent-2411-127: This method of creating morphs leaves very little visual information about the second identity, making it extremely challenging to reconstruct it accurately.
elements.sent-2411-128: As a result, the outputs often closely resemble the morph itself, a phenomenon we refer to as morph-replication.
elements.sent-2411-129: Preprocessing: All images are processed using MTCNN \cite{ref71 to detect faces, after which they are cropped to include only the face regions.}
elements.sent-2411-130: The images are then normalized and resized to a resolution of $256\times256$.
elements.sent-2411-131: We discard the images on which faces cannot be detected.
elements.sent-2411-132: Importantly, no additional spatial transformations are applied to the images.
elements.sent-2411-133: This ensures that the facial features (such as lips, nose, etc.) of both morphs and ground-truth constituent images remain aligned during training.
elements.sent-2411-134: We present the dataset statistics in Table \ref{tab:datasets .}

It's important to note that these transformations are not applied during the training phase.} \label{fig:transformations}
elements.sent-2411-135: Given a morphed face image MORPH as input, our reference-free method generates two output images, OUT1 and OUT2.
elements.sent-2411-136: Since the outputs are unordered, we determine the correct pairing with the ground truth face images, BF1 and BF2, by calculating the similarities for the two possible output-ground truth pairs.
elements.sent-2411-137: We use a face comparator $\mathcal{B}$ to assess facial similarity.
elements.sent-2411-138: If the sum $\mathcal{B (\text{BF1 , \text{OUT1 ) + \mathcal{B (\text{BF2 , \text{OUT2 )$ is greater than $\mathcal{B (\text{BF1 , \text{OUT2 ) + \mathcal{B (\text{BF2 , \text{OUT1 )$, we deem (BF1, OUT1) and (BF2, OUT2) as the correct pairs; otherwise, we swap OUT1 with OUT2.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: For each $\text{OUT _i$, we consider its correct pair $\text{BF _i$ as genuine, where $i,j \in$ \{1,2\} without replacement.}}
elements.sent-2411-140: The imposter score is computed by identifying the closest matching face in the bonafide database, excluding the ground truth image.
elements.sent-2411-141: Note that this evaluation strategy enables us to detect morph-replication.
elements.sent-2411-142: If our method replicates the MORPH as its outputs, i.e., $\mathcal{B (\text{MORPH , \text{OUT1 ) \approx \mathcal{B (\text{MORPH , \text{OUT2 )$, the similarity scores $\mathcal{B (\text{BF1 , \text{OUT1 ) + \mathcal{B (\text{BF2 , \text{OUT2 )$ will also be approximately equal to $\mathcal{B (\text{BF1 , \text{OUT2 ) + \mathcal{B (\text{BF2 , \text{OUT1 )$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: As a result, the pairs (BF1, OUT1) and (BF2, OUT2) would be assigned arbitrarily, causing the match rate to drop close to zero.
elements.sent-2411-144: We evaluate our reference free demorphing method in terms of True Match Rate (TMR) at different thresholds of False Match Rate (FMR).
elements.sent-2411-145: For differential demorphing, we use a modified approach.
elements.sent-2411-146: Given a MORPH and a reference image REF, our method generates an output image, OUT.
elements.sent-2411-147: We treat the ground-truth image BF and OUT as the genuine pair.
elements.sent-2411-148: For the imposter pair, we search the entire test set of bonafide images in the FRLL dataset and assign the face with the third highest similarity to OUT, excluding GT and REF for obvious reasons (hence third).
elements.sent-2411-149: During evaluation, we arbitrarily select one of the constituent face image used to create MORPH as the reference image.
elements.sent-2411-150: However, this does not reflect a real-world scenario, as the exact reference image used to create the morph might not be available during demorphing.
elements.sent-2411-151: To simulate this, we apply one or more random transformations to the reference image (see Figure \ref{fig:transformations ), such as Gaussian blur, jitter, affine transformation, etc. It is important to note that these transformations were not applied during training.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Comparison of our reference-free approach to SDeMorph \cite{ref18} and Identity-Preserving Demorphing (IPD) \cite{ref66}. elements.sent-2411-243: A facial morph is an image strategically created by combining two face images pertaining to two distinct identities.
elements.sent-2411-244: The goal is to create a face image that can be matched to two different identities by a face matcher.
elements.sent-2411-245: Existing demorphing techniques are either very restrictive (assume identities during testing) or produce feeble outputs (both outputs look very similar - the morph-replication problem).
elements.sent-2411-246: In this paper, we overcome these issues by proposing dc-GAN (dual-conditioned GAN), a novel demorphing method conditioned on the morph image as well as the embedding extracted from the image.
elements.sent-2411-247: Code and train-test split are available at hidden for blind review.
elements.sent-2411-248: Morphing, in the biometric literature, refers to the blending of two or more face images to generate a composite image that matches the identities of the images used to create it.
elements.sent-2411-249: Morph Attack Detection (MAD) is essential for retaining the integrity, security and reliability of a biometric system.
elements.sent-2411-250: Reference-based methods have two images: the input face image (say, on the passport) and a reference face image (say, the live image of a person acquired at the port of entry).
elements.sent-2411-251: Reference-free methods have only one image: the input face image.
elements.sent-2411-252: Limited work exists in this field, especially in the reference-free category.
elements.sent-2411-253: In this paper, we focus on both reference-based as well as reference-free face demorphing.
elements.figure-2411-028: \includegraphics[width=0.7\columnwidth]{images/WACV-arch-3.png} \caption{Dual-Conditioned GAN for Reference-Free Demorphing: An image encoder, $\mathcal{E}$, encodes the morph image, which is then used as a condition in the generator. The generator, based on a UNet architecture, $\mathcal{G}$, takes in the MORPH image and the encoded representation, $\mathcal{E}(MORPH)$, producing two outputs, OUT1 and OUT2. The discriminator is trained to distinguish between the real set (MORPH, BF1, BF2) and the synthetic set (MORPH, OUT1, OUT2), differentiating real from synthetic pairs.} \label{fig:arch}

elements.sent-2411-258: We propose dc-GAN, a novel GAN-based technique designed to extract constituent images from a single morph.
elements.sent-2411-259: We conduct a thorough analysis of the implicit bias present in morphs and extensively benchmark publicly available face recognition systems (FRS) on demorphing tasks in the presence of high frequency visible artefacts which arise during demorphing process.
elements.sent-2411-260: To the best of our knowledge, our method is the first to overcome the issue of morph-replication on unseen identities (scenario 3).
elements.sent-2411-261: Face morphing refers to the process of combining two or more face images corresponding to two distinct identities to produce another face image (called a morph) which has a high biometric similarity (or lower distance), as assessed by an automated face matcher.
elements.sent-2411-262: Typically, a morphing operator, $\mathcal{M}$, acts upon two input bonafides, $\mathcal{I _1$ and $\mathcal{I _2$, to produce the morph $\mathcal{X}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{X}=\mathcal{M}(\mathcal{I}_1,\mathcal{I}_2)\end{equation} The goal of $\mathcal{M}$ is to ensure that $\mathcal{B (\mathcal{I _1,\mathcal{X )>\tau$ and $\mathcal{B (\mathcal{I _2,\mathcal{X )>\tau$ where $\mathcal{B}$ is a biometric face matcher that produces a similarity score and $\tau$ is the similarity threshold.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-263: FD-GAN \cite{ref17 is a reference-based method which uses a symmetric GAN architecture and attempts to recover an image of the first identity from the morphed input using an image of the second identity.}
elements.sent-2411-264: Face demorphing is the inverse of the morphing problem.
elements.sent-2411-265: The intricacy lies not only in the absence of the morphing technique used (landmark-based or deep learning based) but also in the lack of constraints on the output space.
elements.sent-2411-266: Given a morph $\mathcal{X}$, the goal of the demorphing operator, denoted as $\mathcal{DM}$ is to recover the constituent face images, \begin{equation}O_1,O_2=\mathcal{DM}(\mathcal{X})\end{equation} satisfying the following conditions: \begin{equation}\label{eq3} ||\mathcal{B}(O_1)-\mathcal{B}(O_2)||^2 <\theta\end{equation} and, \begin{equation}\label{eq4} \max_{j\in\{1,2\}} \max_{\substack{k \in \{1,2\} k \neq j}} (||\mathcal{B}(O_j)-\mathcal{B}(I_k)||^2,||\mathcal{B}(O_j)-\mathcal{B}(I_j)||^2) >\epsilon\end{equation} where $\theta$ and $\epsilon$ are similarity thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-267: Equation \ref{eq3 enforces the reconstructed outputs to look dissimilar among themselves, an issue referred to as morph replication, while Equation \ref{eq4 enforces that each output aligns with its corresponding ground truth image.}}
elements.sent-2411-268: However, the method has a limitation, where the outputs not only closely resemble the morph image but also appear similar to each other.
elements.sent-2411-269: We term this phenomenon as morph-replication.
elements.sent-2411-270: This issue arises because the morphing operator $\mathcal{M}$ is a highly nonlinear function, making demorphing an ill-posed problem.
elements.sent-2411-271: The morph image alone may not be adequate to recover both constituent face images.

elements.sent-2411-281: Face demorphing inverts this process and attempts to recover the original images constituting a facial morph.
elements.sent-2411-282: Existing demorphing techniques have two major limitations: (a) they assume that some identities are common in the train and test sets; and (b) they are prone to the morph replication problem, where the outputs are merely replicates of the input morph.
elements.sent-2411-283: Our method overcomes the morph replication problem and produces high-fidelity reconstructions of the constituent images.
elements.sent-2411-284: Moreover, the proposed method is highly generalizable and applicable to both reference-based and reference-free demorphing methods.
elements.sent-2411-285: Experiments were conducted using the AMSL, FRLL-Morphs, and MorDiff datasets to demonstrate the efficacy of the method.
elements.sent-2411-286: Morphing, in the face recognition literature, refers to the blending of two or more face images to generate a composite image that matches the identities represented in the constituent images with respect to a face matcher.
elements.sent-2411-287: Such a composite image, known as a facial morph, can be incorporated into a biometric-based identity document, thereby allowing multiple individuals to share the same document.
elements.sent-2411-288: This can be used to compromise the security of an application \cite{ref10,ref11 .}
elements.sent-2411-289: Traditionally, morphs were created by extracting facial landmarks from face images and then combining the two face images \cite{ref23,ref21,ref50 .}
elements.sent-2411-290: However, more recently, deep learning models have been remarkably successful in generating high-quality morphs, especially those based on generative paradigms like diffusion models \cite{ref9 and generative adversarial networks \cite{ref13,ref14,ref20 .}}
elements.sent-2411-291: Morph Attack Detection (MAD) is essential for retaining the integrity, security, and reliability of a biometric system \cite{ref10 .}
elements.sent-2411-292: Current MAD techniques can be broadly divided into two categories: i) reference-based differential-image techniques \cite{ref16,ref53 , and ii) reference-free single-image techniques \cite{ref18,ref51,ref41 .}}
elements.sent-2411-293: Reference-based methods use two images: the input face image (say, on the passport) and a reference face image (say, the live image of a person acquired at the port of entry).
elements.sent-2411-294: Reference-free methods use only one image: the input face image.
elements.sent-2411-295: Although MAD techniques can reliably flag facial morphs, they do not reveal the images used to create the morph.
elements.sent-2411-296: From a forensic standpoint, it is valuable to recover images of the constituent identities\footnote{The constituent images are sometimes referred to as ``bonafides" (BF) in the literature, since they are real images of real people from a morph flagged by MAD software.}
elements.sent-2411-297: This process of extracting constituent face images from a morph image is called demorphing.

The generator, based on a UNet architecture, $\mathcal{G}$, takes in the MORPH image and the encoded representation, $\mathcal{E}(MORPH)$, producing two outputs, OUT1 and OUT2. The discriminator is trained to distinguish between the real set (MORPH, BF1, BF2) and the synthetic set (MORPH, OUT1, OUT2), differentiating real from synthetic pairs.} \label{fig:arch}

elements.sent-2411-306: In summary, our contributions are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-307: Our approach is well-suited for both reference-free and differential (i.e., reference-based) demorphing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-308: We conduct a thorough analysis of the implicit bias present in morphs and extensively benchmark publicly available face recognition models on demorphing in the presence of high frequency visible artifacts which arise during the demorphing process.
elements.sent-2411-309: To the best of our knowledge, our method is the first to overcome the issue of morph-replication when test morphs are created using previously unseen identities (scenario 3).
elements.sent-2411-310: Face morphing refers to the process of combining two face images corresponding to two distinct identities to produce another face image (called a morph) which has a high biometric similarity with both constituent identities, as assessed by an automated face matcher.
elements.sent-2411-311: Typically, a morphing operator, $\mathcal{M}$, acts upon two input bonafides, $\mathcal{I _1$ and $\mathcal{I _2$, to produce the morph $\mathcal{X}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{X}=\mathcal{M}(\mathcal{I}_1,\mathcal{I}_2)\end{equation} The goal of $\mathcal{M}$ is to ensure that $\mathcal{B (\mathcal{I _1,\mathcal{X )>\tau$ and $\mathcal{B (\mathcal{I _2,\mathcal{X )>\tau$, where, $\mathcal{B}$ is a biometric face matcher that produces a similarity score and $\tau$ is the similarity threshold.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-371: In this paper, we propose dc-GAN, a novel method to recover constituent images from a facial morph.
elements.sent-2411-372: Our proposed method is based on the GAN architecture; we condition our GAN on morphs as well as their latent representations to provide robust guidance while recovering constituent images.
elements.sent-2411-373: Previous approaches suffered from morph-replication, i.e., they produced similar looking faces as the morph itself.
elements.sent-2411-374: Our method significantly overcomes this problem.
elements.sent-2411-375: We evaluate our method across 6 different morphing techniques using five biometric face models.
elements.sent-2411-376: We assess our method using the AMSL, FRLL-Morphs, and MorDiff datasets, achieving visually compelling results and addressing the issue of morph-replication.
elements.sent-2411-377: Moreover, experiments suggest that ArcFace performs consistently for demorphing tasks compared to other face recognition models.
elements.sent-2411-378: Future work will aim to develop techniques for demorphing faces with various styles, beyond just passport-style face images.
elements.sent-2411-379: This work was supported in part by the NSF Center for Identification Technology Research (CITeR) and the DHS Center for Criminal Investigations and Network Analysis (CINA).
elements.sent-2411-380: All experiments were carried out using existing face datasets available for research purposes, and no attempts were made to identify individuals within these datasets.
elements.sent-2411-381: Additionally, no personally identifiable information (PII) was used during the demorphing process described in this paper.. { \balance \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}}
